skill.name: multiplayer
skill.description: Guidance for choosing multiplayer game architecture, synchronization methods, network optimization, security validation, and matchmaking tradeoffs.
skill.summary: Plan multiplayer game networking with practical architecture and synchronization guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but the referenced lines are Markdown diagrams and game design guidance. No evidence found of executable commands, malicious intent, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or unsafe cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Lines 17-31 and 90-95 are fenced Markdown diagrams that describe multiplayer architecture and server validation. They do not contain shell commands or executable Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations are plain Markdown code fences with decision tree text and validation examples. No command syntax or execution instruction is present.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Lines 3 and 127 discuss multiplayer networking and latency design. No weak cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, encryption call, or security implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line content is descriptive game development guidance. There is no cryptographic API usage or recommendation to use a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Lines 92-93 describe server-side validation questions for game events. They do not instruct host, network, account, or environment reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text is a validation checklist inside a game security section. It has no commands or requests for system information.

low_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions Require Publisher Awareness
low_findings.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, and Edit access in its front matter. The body does not ask the assistant to run commands, so this is a permission hygiene concern rather than active malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission declaration is explicit, but no risky action is requested by the skill content. Reviewers may still prefer least-privilege tool declarations.

content.value_statement: Multiplayer features often fail because architecture and synchronization choices are unclear. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reason through networking tradeoffs before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Compares dedicated, peer-to-peer, host-based, and distributed server architectures.
Explains state synchronization, input synchronization, hybrid models, and lag compensation.
Lists bandwidth optimization methods such as delta compression and area of interest filtering.
Defines recommended update rates for position, health, inventory, and chat data.
Summarizes server authority, anti-cheat validation, and matchmaking considerations.
limitations: Does not include production-ready networking code or engine-specific integrations.
Does not benchmark transport protocols, cloud providers, or relay services.
Does not cover persistence, account systems, payments, or live operations.
Requires developers to adapt guidance to each game engine and genre.

use_cases.title: Choose a Networking Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare server models for a new competitive, cooperative, turn-based, or massive multiplayer project.

use_cases.title: Plan Synchronization Behavior
use_cases.description: Decide when to use prediction, interpolation, reconciliation, and server lag compensation.

use_cases.title: Review Multiplayer Security
use_cases.description: Check whether the server validates movement, hits, inventory, visibility, and timing.

prompt_templates.title: Pick an Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new multiplayer game.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a multiplayer architecture for a game with this genre, player count, latency target, and budget.

prompt_templates.title: Design State Sync
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my multiplayer entities and recommend what should use state sync, input sync, or a hybrid model.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Bandwidth
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this multiplayer update model and suggest bandwidth reductions using priority, quantization, deltas, and area of interest.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Server Authority
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my multiplayer security model and identify where the server must validate client claims.

output_examples.input: A two-player cooperative action game needs online play with low infrastructure cost.
output_examples.output: The likely recommendation is host-based networking with clear host migration limits, server-side validation where possible, and interpolation for remote movement.

output_examples.input: A competitive shooter has poor hit registration for players with 120 ms latency.
output_examples.output: The response would focus on client prediction, reconciliation, interpolation buffers, authoritative hit validation, and bounded server rewind.

output_examples.input: An MMO prototype sends every entity update to every player.
output_examples.output: The response would recommend area of interest filtering, update prioritization, lower rates for low-value data, and distributed server planning.

best_practices: Start with the game genre, player count, trust model, and latency budget.
Keep the server authoritative for movement, combat, inventory, and rewards.
Plan bandwidth limits before adding high-frequency replicated objects.
anti_patterns: Do not trust client claims for hits, movement, currency, or inventory.
Do not send hidden or irrelevant world data to every player.
Do not ignore latency until late testing.

faq.question: Does this skill write multiplayer networking code?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning guidance and review criteria for architecture, synchronization, optimization, security, and matchmaking.

faq.question: Which game engines does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance is engine-neutral. Developers can apply it to Unity, Unreal, Godot, custom engines, or web games.

faq.question: Can it help with anti-cheat design?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes server authority and validation of movement, hits, inventory, visibility, and timing.

faq.question: Does it cover matchmaking?
faq.answer: Yes. It lists key factors such as skill, latency, wait time, and party size.

faq.question: Is this enough for production deployment?
faq.answer: No. Production systems also need protocol choices, scaling plans, monitoring, persistence, and operational testing.

faq.question: Why is the security risk low?
faq.answer: The skill contains Markdown guidance only. Static command and cryptography findings were false positives, but broad tool permissions remain visible.
